title: How to Buy Prescription Glasses Online: Step-by-Step Guide
description: Buying glasses online? Follow these steps to get your prescription right, measure PD accurately, choose the right lenses, and avoid common mistakes.
tldr: To buy glasses online, you need a current prescription (less than 2 years old), your PD measurement (54-74mm for adults), and knowledge of what frame size fits you. Online glasses are cheapest for simple single vision prescriptions but riskier for progressives or strong prescriptions. The main tradeoff is price vs. fitting accuracy — you save money but sacrifice the professional fitting that ensures lenses are properly centred for your eyes.

tldrStats.label: Best lens type for online orders

definition.text: The process of ordering prescription eyeglasses through an online retailer. Requires the buyer to provide their own prescription, PD measurement, and frame size preferences. Lenses are manufactured remotely and shipped to the customer without in-person fitting or verification.

sections.content:
You need a valid, up-to-date prescription from an optometrist or ophthalmologist. In most Canadian provinces, prescriptions are valid for 2 years for adults and 1 year for children.

Your prescription should include:

  • SPH (Sphere): The main correction power for each eye
  • CYL (Cylinder) and AXIS: Astigmatism correction (if applicable)
  • ADD: Near-vision addition power (for progressive or bifocal lenses, if applicable)

If your prescription is expired or you don't have one, you'll need an eye exam first. In Alberta, exams for adults typically cost $75-$150 and are usually covered by employer insurance. You're legally entitled to receive a copy of your prescription after the exam — ask for it explicitly.

Note: online retailers legally cannot fill an expired prescription. Don't try to use old numbers — your vision likely changed.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

PD is the distance between your pupils in millimeters. Online retailers need this to centre the lenses in front of your eyes. Most prescriptions from optometrists don't include PD because it's considered a fitting measurement, not a prescription value.

How to measure at home:

  1. Stand 20cm from a mirror in good lighting
  2. Hold a millimetre ruler flat against your brow ridge
  3. Close your right eye and align 0mm with the centre of your left pupil
  4. Without moving the ruler, close your left eye and open your right
  5. Read the number at the centre of your right pupil
  6. Repeat 3 times and average for accuracy

Average adult PD is 58-68mm. If your number falls outside 52-75mm, re-measure — you likely made an error.

The accuracy problem: Self-measured PD can be off by 2-3mm. For mild single vision prescriptions, this is usually tolerable. For prescriptions above +/-4.00 or for progressive lenses, a 2mm PD error causes noticeable eye strain and headaches. For complex prescriptions, get PD measured professionally at an optical store before ordering online.

Online frames have three key measurements (printed on the inside temple of your current glasses):

  • Lens width: First number (e.g., 52mm). Determines how large the lens opening is.
  • Bridge width: Second number (e.g., 18mm). How wide the nose bridge is. Must match your nose width for comfort.
  • Temple length: Third number (e.g., 140mm). Length of the arm from hinge to ear tip.

If you have current glasses that fit well, copy those measurements exactly. If not, you're guessing — and frame sizing is one of the biggest reasons online glasses purchases fail.

Virtual try-on tools help with style selection but are unreliable for fit assessment. They can't account for nose bridge shape, ear position, or weight distribution. If you've never worn glasses before, buying your first pair online is risky. Start with an in-person fitting to learn your dimensions, then use those measurements for future online orders.

Online retailers offer a confusing array of lens options. Here's what matters:

  • Lens material: Standard plastic (1.50 index) for mild prescriptions. Polycarbonate for kids or safety. High-index (1.60-1.74) for prescriptions above +/-3.00 — thinner and lighter.
  • Anti-reflective coating: Worth the $15-$40 upgrade on every pair. Reduces glare and makes lenses nearly invisible.
  • Scratch-resistant coating: Usually included free or for $10-$15. Always add it.
  • Blue light filtering: Optional ($10-$30). May help with screen comfort. Not essential.
  • Photochromic (Transitions): Lenses that darken in sunlight ($40-$100 extra). Convenient but can't replace dedicated sunglasses for driving.

Which lens type works online?

Lens TypeOnline Success RateWhy
Single vision (mild Rx)HighForgiving of small PD errors
Single vision (strong Rx)ModeratePD accuracy matters more
ProgressiveLowRequires precise PD + segment height + fitting
BifocalLow-ModerateSegment height critical

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

Popular online options in Canada:

  • Clearly (clearly.ca): Canadian-based, wide frame selection, free shipping and returns. Owned by EssilorLuxottica. Best online option for Canadians.
  • Zenni Optical: Budget champion — complete glasses from $15-$50 USD. Quality varies. Good for backup pairs.
  • Warby Parker: Style-focused with a home try-on program. Mid-range pricing. Expanding in Canada.
  • EyeBuyDirect: Budget to mid-range. Owned by EssilorLuxottica. Frequent promotions.

Before you order, check:

  • Return policy — most reputable retailers offer 30-day returns
  • Warranty — look for at least 1-year coverage on defects
  • Lens manufacturer — branded lenses (Essilor, Zeiss) are more consistent than unbranded
  • Canadian shipping — some US retailers charge duties or slow shipping fees

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

Online glasses work best for specific situations. In these cases, you're better off visiting an optical store:

  • First-time glasses wearer: You don't know your face measurements, your frame preferences, or what prescription feels right. An optician walks you through all of it.
  • Progressive lenses: The fitting involves PD, segment height, vertex distance, and pantoscopic tilt — measurements that can't be self-taken accurately.
  • Strong prescriptions (above +/-4.00): PD errors at high powers cause significant prismatic effects. Professional measurement is essential.
  • Astigmatism above -1.50 CYL: Axis accuracy and lens centering matter more with higher cylinder values.
  • Need them quickly: Online orders take 1-3 weeks. Same-day glasses are available in-store for single vision prescriptions.

Many people use a hybrid approach: primary everyday glasses from a professional optical store with perfect fitting, and a backup pair ordered online. You get the best of both worlds — quality primary glasses and an affordable spare for $50-$80. For your primary pair, stores like Charm Optical in Edmonton provide expert fitting, designer brands, and direct insurance billing.

faqs.q: Is it safe to buy prescription glasses online?
faqs.a: It's safe for simple single vision prescriptions where you know your measurements. For progressive lenses, strong prescriptions, or first-time wearers, in-store fitting is significantly more reliable. Reputable online retailers use the same lens materials as physical stores.

faqs.q: How do I get my PD for online glasses?
faqs.a: Measure at home using a millimetre ruler in a mirror, or ask your optometrist's office for the measurement. Some optical stores will measure PD for a small fee. For progressive lenses, always get professional measurement — self-measured PD isn't accurate enough.

faqs.q: Can I use my insurance for online glasses?
faqs.a: Most insurance plans reimburse online purchases, but you'll need to pay upfront and submit a claim. In-store, many optical stores direct-bill so you only pay the difference. Check with your insurance provider about their reimbursement process for online purchases.

faqs.q: Why are online glasses so much cheaper?
faqs.a: Online retailers skip the overhead of physical stores (rent, in-store opticians, fitting equipment) and often use lower-cost lens manufacturers. The quality tradeoff is in fitting precision and after-care — no free adjustments, no walk-in repairs, no professional verification of lens accuracy.

faqs.q: What if online glasses don't fit?
faqs.a: Most reputable online retailers offer 30-day returns. Some optical stores will adjust online-purchased frames for a small fee. If the prescription feels wrong (blurry, headaches, eye strain), the issue is likely PD or prescription error — contact the retailer for a remake.

ctaText: Whether you buy online or in-store, Charm Optical offers eye exams, PD measurements, and professional fitting in South Edmonton. Walk-ins welcome.
